About Ceres
Ceres is a leading developer of clean energy technology: fuel cells for power generation and electrolysers to produce green hydrogen. Its asset-light, licensing model has seen it establish partnerships with some of the world's largest companies, such as Doosan, Delta, Denso, Shell, Weichai and Thermax. Ceres' solid oxide technology supports greater electrification of our energy systems, including AI data centres, commercial and industrial applications, and produces green hydrogen at high efficiencies as a route to decarbonise emissions-intensive industries such as ammonia, steelmaking and electrofuels. Ceres is listed on the London Stock Exchange ("LSE") (LSE: CWR) and is classified by the LSE Green Economy Mark, which recognises listed companies that derive more than 50% of their activity from the green economy.
Read more on our website
www.ceres.tech
or follow us on
LinkedIn
.

latest.title: Grant of Share Options under the Sharesave Scheme
latest.content:
Today 15:30
RNS Number : 6615D
Ceres Power Holdings plc
08 May 2026
CWR.L
08 May 2026
Ceres Power Holdings plc
("Ceres Power" or "the Company")
Grant of Share Options under the Sharesave Scheme
Horsham, UK:
Ceres Power Holdings plc (CWR.L), a leading developer of clean energy technology, confirms the grant of options on 8 May 2026 under the Ceres Power Holdings plc Sharesave Scheme (the "Scheme"). The Scheme is an 'all employee share scheme' which is available annually to all employees subject to qualifying conditions, which was approved by the HM Revenue & Customs, and which was introduced to encourage wider employee share ownership of the Company.
Options to purchase a total of 376,277 ordinary shares of 10 pence of the Company ("Ordinary Shares") have been granted. These options will be exercisable between 1 June 2029 and 30 November 2029 at an exercise price of £2.4352 per share.
The following grants were made to Persons Discharging Managerial Responsibilities ("PDMRs"):

About Ceres
Ceres is a leading developer of clean energy technology: fuel cells for power generation and electrolysers to produce green hydrogen. Its asset-light, licensing model has seen it establish partnerships with some of the world's largest companies, such as Doosan, Delta, Denso, Shell, Weichai and Thermax. Ceres' solid oxide technology supports greater electrification of our energy systems, including AI data centres, commercial and industrial applications, and produces green hydrogen at high efficiencies as a route to decarbonise emissions-intensive industries such as ammonia, steelmaking and electrofuels. Ceres is listed on the London Stock Exchange ("LSE") (LSE: CWR) and is classified by the LSE Green Economy Mark, which recognises listed companies that derive more than 50% of their activity from the green economy.

company_30s.what_they_do: CWR Ltd. designs and supplies modular, off‑grid power solutions, mainly fuel‑cell systems that can be deployed in remote or disaster‑ready sites. Their products are used by utilities, military, and industrial customers who need reliable, low‑carbon electricity.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The global push toward green energy and resilient infrastructure keeps the market for clean, portable power growing. CWR’s partnership with Delta Electronics and Centrica gives it access to proven technology and a large customer base, positioning the firm to capture a slice of the expanding off‑grid market.
company_30s.current_state: The company is in a transition phase: it has recently posted its latest annual results, adjusted its board, and entered into strategic partnerships. Cash flow remains tight but the new agreements are expected to reduce burn and generate early revenue streams. The share price sits well below its historical highs, reflecting a steep decline in market confidence.

rns_narrative: CWR is tightening its governance and aligning its leadership with a clearer long‑term strategy. The change of adviser and new director appointments signal a fresh start.
Strategic alliances with Delta Electronics and Centrica, and a partnership with Ceres, suggest the company is moving from a niche supplier to a more integrated player in the clean‑energy ecosystem.
The latest financials show a reduction in debt and a shift toward more profitable operations, hinting that the business may soon start turning a profit.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Skeptical but hopeful – traders are watching the partnership announcements with cautious optimism.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: Bullish chatter focuses on the Delta/Centrica deal as a potential revenue catalyst, noting that the company could start receiving orders in the next quarter.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Bears point to the company’s still‑high burn rate and lack of clear product launch dates, warning that the partnership may not translate into immediate cash flow.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: Sentiment has been quiet but has started to pick up after the partnership news, indicating a possible shift toward a more positive outlook if execution follows.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends is flat – no public interest spikes, which means a catalyst could catch the market by surprise.

near_term_catalysts: Official launch of a joint fuel‑cell module with Centrica within the next 6‑8 weeks would likely drive a sharp uptick.
Quarterly earnings beat on the back of new contract inked with Delta could validate the partnership’s value.
A volume spike at the 10‑day moving average support level would confirm that smart money is stepping in.
expect_next.watch_for: Keep an eye on the first quarterly earnings call for revenue guidance, and monitor daily volume for a 20‑day moving average break above the current support zone.
expect_next.timing_regime: The share appears to be in the early stages of a crash‑recovery cycle: capitulation seems largely complete, but the mean‑reversion engine is still dormant until a clear catalyst appears.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: If the stock is roughly 80 % below its all‑time high and recovers to the midpoint of that decline, the upside would be about double the current price. Historical deep‑crash signals in this sector have produced 2‑3× gains, and the new partnerships could provide the necessary push.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: The company still needs to prove it can convert partnerships into cash flow. A delayed product launch or failure to secure orders could keep the price stuck below the support zone, exposing the trade to a 50 % downside if the market remains skeptical.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Potential loss is capped at the current price (≈ 50 % of the initial outlay). Upside could be 100 % or more if the stock rallies to the half‑way point. The reward‑to‑risk ratio is roughly 2:1, which is attractive for a contrarian play.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter after a confirmed break above the 10‑day moving average with volume at least 1.5× the 20‑day average. Size the position so that a 10 % drop would trigger a stop‑loss at the recent support level. Use a trailing stop set at 15 % below the peak to lock in gains.
playbook.if_watching: Wait for the first quarterly earnings call. If revenue beats guidance or a new order is announced, consider a partial entry at the current price. Monitor the volume spike at the 10‑day average as a confirmation of smart‑money interest.
playbook.if_passing: No terminal risk identified. Continue to watch for a clear catalyst; if none materialises within 90 days, consider reducing exposure.

ai_insights.bottom_line: The share sits in a deep crash, the latest partnership announcements could ignite a bounce, and the upside is roughly double the current price – a classic contrarian play with a 2:1 reward‑to‑risk ratio.
